Overall, I would rate my feelings about my life as a researcher as very positive, despite the challenges and pressures that come with the role. I find the work intellectually fulfilling, and I value the contributions I am able to make through my publications and ongoing projects. The main cause of these positive feelings is the sense of purpose and accomplishment that comes from conducting meaningful research and successfully disseminating my findings. As a senior researcher with substantial publishing experience over the past five years, I feel motivated by the impact of my work, the opportunities for continuous learning, and the recognition that comes with scholarly productivity. Even though the pressure to maintain a high level of output can be intense at times, the overall satisfaction and intellectual engagement outweigh the difficulties.

I selected “neutral” because my overall feelings about my life as a researcher oscillate between extremely positive and extremely negative periods. On the positive side, I genuinely enjoy my work: I find research intellectually stimulating and meaningful, I appreciate several of my colleagues, and I value my relationship with students, which I find motivating and rewarding. On the negative side, I experience significant frustration due to administrative burdens and the difficulty of securing funding for my research. There is also considerable stress associated with not always being able to implement projects as planned, and the presence of certain toxic individuals within my institution negatively affects my overall experience.

I experience fluctuating emotions. Sometimes, especially when I submit a research paper to a journal, I feel hopeful, but then the process takes too long, and I lose enthusiasm. Sometimes, revisions come in, I make the peer review corrections, and I think it will be accepted, and I become hopeful again. However, after the revisions, the paper is suddenly rejected. Despair overwhelms me. Over time, this feeling disappears, and I submit the paper to a new journal. The process continues in this vicious cycle.
